OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess and diagnose the patient presenting with hematuria, abdominal discomfort, and bilateral flank masses.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient presents with abdominal discomfort and visible blood in the urine for the past 2 days. He reports having similar episodes of abdominal discomfort intermittently over the past 5 years, although he does not recall prior hematuria. He has a known history of hypertension for the past 2 years and is currently on antihypertensive medication. He denies weight loss, skin rashes, joint pain, vomiting, changes in bowel habits, or smoking.
Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: Hypertension for 2 years on medication. No known autoimmune disease or malignancy.
Patient_Actor.Social_History: Non-smoker. No significant occupational or environmental exposures reported.
Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: Denies fever, vomiting, bowel habit changes, skin rashes, joint pain, or weight loss.

Kidney_Examination.Abdominal_Examination: Normal bowel sounds, no abdominal tenderness

Ultrasonography.Findings: Bilaterally enlarged kidneys with multiple anechoic cysts having well-defined walls

CT_Scan.Findings: Multiple cysts present in both kidneys

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess and diagnose a term newborn presenting shortly after birth with feeding intolerance and bilious vomiting, in the context of dysmorphic features and abnormal abdominal findings.

Patient_Actor.Demographics: 6-hour-old male newborn, birth weight 3050 g (6 lb 12 oz)
Patient_Actor.History: The infant was born at term to a 35-year-old woman via normal vaginal delivery. Pregnancy was largely uncomplicated, though several prenatal checkups were missed. Six hours after delivery, the infant developed feeding intolerance and multiple episodes of bilious vomiting. There is a family history of pyloric stenosis in an older sibling.
Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: Newborn with no prior medical conditions.
Patient_Actor.Family_History: Older brother had hypertrophic pyloric stenosis requiring surgery as an infant.
Patient_Actor.Social_History: No relevant environmental or social risk factors identified.
Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: No respiratory distress noted. Feeding intolerance and gastrointestinal symptoms predominate.

Vital_Signs.Blood_Pressure: Not routinely assessed in this age group

Cardiovascular_Examination.Auscultation: Grade 2/6 holosystolic murmur at the left mid to lower sternal border

Abdominal_Examination.Inspection: Distended upper abdomen with concave-shaped lower abdomen

Abdominal_X-ray.Findings: Characteristic double-bubble appearance with air in the stomach and proximal duodenum and absence of distal bowel gas, consistent with proximal intestinal obstruction.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess and diagnose the patient presenting with seizure and altered mental status.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient was brought to the emergency department after experiencing a generalized tonic-clonic seizure. According to his son, the patient has no prior history of seizures but has had progressive confusion and generalized weakness over the past several weeks.
Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: No known history of seizures. Other past medical history is not specified.
Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: No additional complaints reported beyond neurological symptoms.

Neurological_Examination.Cranial_Nerves: Pupils round and reactive to light and accommodation.

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess and diagnose the newborn with specific findings on physical examination and history provided.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient is a 3000-g (6.6-lb) female newborn delivered at term to a 23-year-old primigravida mother. The mother reports having had no prenatal care, and immunization records for the newborn are not available.

Secondary_Symptoms: Bluish macules on the skin that do not blanch with pressure
Cloudy lenses in both eyes noticed upon examination
Failure to pass auditory screening tests

Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: Newborn, no past medical history available.
Patient_Actor.Social_History: Mother is a primigravida, no prenatal care received.
Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: No additional symptoms noted by the mother.

Skin_Examination.Macules: Several bluish macules that do not blanch upon application of pressure

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Assess and diagnose the infant brought in by a concerned mother due to bilious vomiting and inability to pass meconium, with observed abdominal distension.

Patient_Actor.History: The infant was born via a home birth without complications. The mother reports that the infant has had bilious vomiting and has not been able to pass meconium since birth. She also notes that the infant's abdomen appears distended.
Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: Newborn, no prior medical history. Birth was uneventful.
Patient_Actor.Social_History: The infant is the first child of the family. No known family history of genetic or congenital diseases.
Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: The infant shows signs of distress, possibly due to abdominal discomfort.

Abdominal_Examination.Palpation: The abdomen is firm and distended, with palpable bowel loops; the infant shows signs of discomfort during palpation.

Abdominal_X-ray.Findings: Dilated loops of bowel with no gas in the rectum, suggestive of a distal obstruction.

Rectal_Biopsy.Histopathology: Absence of ganglion cells in the submucosa and myenteric plexus

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Evaluate a newborn with seizures, feeding difficulty, and abnormal physical findings to determine the underlying cause.

Patient_Actor.History: A 2-day-old male newborn delivered vaginally at 36 weeks gestation developed generalized convulsions lasting approximately 2 minutes. Prior to the event, he was lethargic and had difficulty feeding. Pregnancy and delivery were uncomplicated, with Apgar scores of 7 and 8 at 1 and 5 minutes, respectively.
Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: No known medical conditions; mother took folic acid and a multivitamin; maternal immunizations up to date.
Patient_Actor.Social_History: Third pregnancy of a 29-year-old mother; previous pregnancies and deliveries uncomplicated.

Auditory_Screening.Findings: Did not pass auditory screening tests

Cranial_Ultrasonography.Findings: Ventricular dilatation and hyperechoic foci within the cortex, basal ganglia, and periventricular region

OSCE_Examination.Objective_for_Doctor: Diagnose the underlying condition based on clinical presentation and examination findings in an 8-month-old boy with feeding difficulties and abdominal distension.

Patient_Actor.History: The patient has been very fussy and has shown decreased interest in feeding for the past few days. His mother reports that despite the feeding difficulties, she believes the child has been gaining weight. There were no complications during birth at 39 weeks of gestation.

Symptoms.Primary_Symptom: Crying, especially intense with abdominal palpation

Patient_Actor.Past_Medical_History: No significant past medical or surgical history. Delivered vaginally at 39 weeks without complications.
Patient_Actor.Social_History: First child to young parents, no history of similar illness in the family.
Patient_Actor.Review_of_Systems: No episodes of fever, vomiting, or diarrhea. The baby has not had any known illnesses since birth.

Abdominal_Examination.Percussion: Tympanitic sound noted in the left lower quadrant
Abdominal_Examination.Palpation: Abdomen is soft but distended, with crying intensifying on palpation especially in the left lower quadrant

Abdominal_X-ray.Findings: Dilated bowel segments with absence of gas in the rectum, suggesting a possible obstruction

Barium_Enema.Findings: A transition zone in the distal colon, compatible with Hirschsprung disease
